title: Source-independent quantum key distribution without pre-sending entanglement
abstract: Quantum key distribution (QKD) theoretically offers information-theoretic security. The prevailing approach is the prepare-and-measure BB84 protocol, which implements QKD using conventional laser rather than single-photon source via the decoy-state method. However, side-channel attacks targeting sources severely threaten system security. Despite extensive efforts, including fully passive scheme, this vulnerability persists even with perfect single-photon source. Here, we propose a source-independent (SI) QKD protocol that resolves all known and unknown source-side attacks without pre-sending entanglement source. Aligning with advances in quantum light sources, our protocol simultaneously doubles the transmission distance while remaining robustness against imperfection of source. Theoretical analysis shows that non-classical light source provides practical security advantages unattainable with conventional laser.
summary: This paper introduces a new source-independent QKD protocol that is immune to all known and unknown source-side attacks without needing to pre-send entanglement. By using non-classical light sources, the protocol can double the transmission distance compared to standard single-photon BB84. The study demonstrates that this approach is robust against source imperfections and provides a practical path for long-distance quantum networks.
main_problem: The vulnerability of QKD protocols to source-side attacks (e.g., photon-number splitting, light-injection) and the high experimental complexity of existing source-independent protocols that require pre-sending entanglement.
main_result: The proposed SI-QKD protocol eliminates all known and unknown source-side attacks without pre-sending entanglement, while simultaneously doubling the transmission distance and outperforming conventional WCS-based decoy-state protocols.
method: The authors propose a protocol using non-classical light sources (single-photon or odd cat states) where an untrusted third party interferes pulses at a PBS, followed by a security analysis using the entropy uncertainty relation and finite-size key rate calculations.
model_or_system: A source-independent QKD setup where an untrusted third party (Charlie) uses independent non-classical sources or a single source with an active optical switch to inject pulses into a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) for distribution to Alice and Bob.
key_observables: Secure key rate (SKR), quantum bit error rate (QBER), phase error rate (phi_z), and the second-order correlation function g(2)(0).
important_parameters: Average photon number (n), second-order correlation function g(2)(0), channel attenuation (0.16 dB/km), detector efficiency (0.8), and dark count rate (10^-7 to 10^-5).
main_assumptions: The quantum source is treated as an untrusted black box; local measurements at Alice and Bob are assumed to be trusted and well-characterized; the channel is assumed to be symmetric.
figures_summary: Fig 3(a) compares finite SKR of the proposed protocol against BB84 variants; Fig 3(b) investigates performance; Fig 4 shows g(2)(0) and photon number as functions of intensity; Fig 5 shows the optical switch schematic; Fig 6 shows SKR vs. distance for different dark count rates and data sizes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of source-side vulnerabilities, proposes a new SI-QKD protocol using non-classical light, provides a theoretical security framework based on uncertainty relations, presents numerical simulations comparing performance and distance limits, and discusses experimental implementation via active optical switching.
why_interesting: It presents a significant advancement in quantum optics and quantum communication by leveraging non-classical states (like cat states) to achieve higher security and longer transmission distances without the overhead of entanglement distribution.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Protocols comparison. (a) Single-photon based and (b) WCS-based BB84 protocols: both the source and measurement devices must be trusted. (c) SI-QKD protocol: The non-classical source generation and distribution are mod- eled as an untrusted black box, while the local measurements at Alice and Bob are trusted. PM: phase modulator, IM: in- tensity modulator, BS: beam splitter.

FIG. 2. The polarization scheme of SI-QKD protocol. Charlie uses two independent non-classical sources that each emits a sequence of optical pulses. Each photon is prepared in the |+⟩state by a polarizer (Pol) and half-wave plate (HWP). Pulses from two sources interfere at a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) and distributed to receivers. Alice and Bob use a local beam splitter (BS) to randomly select the measurement basis, followed by polarization analysis in either the rectilinear (Z) basis or the diagonal (X ) basis. Measurement outcomes are recorded by four single-photon detectors (SPDs) corresponding to polarization bases.

FIG. 3. Comparison of finite secure key rate. (a) Fi- nite SKR comparison between our polarization SI-QKD pro- tocol (solid lines), the single-photon BB84 protocol [53] (dash- dotted lines), and the WCS-based decoy-state BB84 proto- col [57] (dashed lines). The parameters are set as ed = 0.01, pd = 10−7 and N = 1012. (b) Finite SKR for various mis- alignment error ed with fixed parameters g(2)(0) = 0.01. The transmission distance denotes the fiber length between Alice and Bob.
FIG. 4. Characterization of odd cat state. (a) Second- order function g(2)(0) as a function of the intensity µ = |α|2

FIG. 5. The experimental setup of optical switch. Sin- gle photons enter the Sagnac interferometer via the circulator (Cir) and beam splitter (BS). An electro-optic phase modu- lator (PM) applies periodic phase shifts at specific time slots. Depending on the relative phase (π or 0), photons from odd and even time slots are routed to separate output ports (c and d).
